indicator_text: Who is lobbying and who is paying for it (lobbyist and client identification, with separate registrations preferred)
section_or_category: 1. Who is lobbying and who is paying for it?
scoring_rule: Five-point scale (0-5). Baseline = 3 (states identify both lobbyist and client). 4 = states require separate registrations for lobbyists and clients. Below baseline (0-3) per individual circumstances. (Maximum 5 implied by category cap; descriptors 4 and below explicit in text.)
source_quote: the baseline practice is identification of both lobbyist and client. States that separate registrations for lobbyists and clients, as opposed to the practice of lobbyists registering and including the names of their clients within, are scored higher. papers/text/OpenSecrets_2022__state_lobbying_disclosure_scorecard.txt:49-55
notes: Category 1 of 4. Article: "For lobbying/client disclosure, the baseline score was three and states that require separate registrations for the lobbyists and clients were assigned a four" (line 197-199). The text does not explicitly describe the score-5 anchor for this category. Rationale for separate registration preference is data-quality / single-source-of-truth (lines 60-73).

indicator_text: How much are lobbyists getting paid (compensation paid to lobbyists by clients)
section_or_category: 2. How much are lobbyists getting paid?
scoring_rule: Five-point scale (0-5). Baseline practice scored 4. Exceeding baseline scored 5. Below baseline 0-3: states that do not require disclosure of compensation = 0. Partial compensation disclosure (compensation in ranges or not linked to individual lobbyists) scored 1-3 "in relation to the level of compensation disclosed."
source_quote: For compensation and timely reporting, a score of four was assigned to those states that are at least requiring the baseline practices discussed above and a score of five for those that are exceeding the baseline... states that do not require disclosure of compensation received a score of zero for that category. papers/text/OpenSecrets_2022__state_lobbying_disclosure_scorecard.txt:199-209
notes: Category 2 of 4. Identified by OpenSecrets as the single most important indicator: "the key factor in OpenSecrets' ability to collect and display data on state lobbying spending" (line 82-83); compensation = 84% of the $1.8B tracked in 2021 (line 85-87). Text does not explicitly state the "baseline" for compensation; from context = full compensation disclosure linked to individual lobbyists. 17 states require no compensation disclosure; 7 require partial; 26 require full disclosure (lines 78-80, 266-267).

indicator_text: Is there timely disclosure (frequency/timing of lobbying activity reports)
scoring_rule: Five-point scale (0-5). Baseline practice scored 4 = monthly reporting when legislature is in session, quarterly otherwise. Exceeding baseline scored 5 = monthly reports throughout the year. Below baseline 0-3 per individual circumstances (e.g., once-yearly reporting yields very low scores).
source_quote: In our scoring system, the baseline for timely disclosure is set to monthly reporting when the legislature is in session, and quarterly otherwise. This is a standard that 20 states are already using some variation of. papers/text/OpenSecrets_2022__state_lobbying_disclosure_scorecard.txt:98-101
notes: Category 3 of 4. Top-scoring states "had perfect scores in timely disclosure, requiring monthly reports throughout the year" (line 226-227). Bottom states (ND, SD) "only require reporting once a year" (line 235-236). 16 states require some form of monthly reporting (line 279-280). 5 = monthly year-round; 4 = monthly in-session/quarterly otherwise (baseline); below = less frequent.

indicator_text: How easily can the public access disclosed information (composite of three sub-facets, total 5 points)
section_or_category: 4. How easily can the public access disclosed information?
scoring_rule: Five-point composite (0-5) summed from three sub-facets: (a) user-friendly search = 2 points, (b) availability of downloadable data = 2 points, (c) availability of lobbyist and client lists = 1 point. Partial points allowed for partially-accessible lists.
source_quote: Each of these factors is considered a best practice for public availability and are combined to determine a total public availability score for each state... Within the five points allocated to this category, one point is dedicated to the availability of lobbyist and client lists. Partial points were assigned based on lists that are available, but not as easily accessed. Two points each were assigned to user-friendly search and availability of downloadable data. papers/text/OpenSecrets_2022__state_lobbying_disclosure_scorecard.txt:110-216
notes: Category 4 of 4. Composite indicator with three named sub-facets enumerated in narrative as "three key facets" (lines 106-109). Sub-facets are atomized as separate indicator rows below (public_avail_search, public_avail_downloads, public_avail_lists). OpenSecrets describes this category as "more subjective" than the others (line 211).

indicator_text: User-friendly search feature on state lobbying disclosure website
section_or_category: 4. How easily can the public access disclosed information? (sub-facet a)
scoring_rule: 0-2 points (sub-component of category 4). 2 = user-friendly search. Subjective scoring; partial points implied.
source_quote: Two points each were assigned to user-friendly search and availability of downloadable data. papers/text/OpenSecrets_2022__state_lobbying_disclosure_scorecard.txt:215-216
notes: Sub-facet of public_availability_total. Article rationale: "A search is not particularly helpful if it is buried behind several click throughs, filled with jargon that only makes sense to insiders or requires prior knowledge to perform the search" (lines 117-119). Recommendations include don't hide search behind menus, replace jargon, auto-populate lists (lines 290-297).

indicator_text: Availability of downloadable data from the state lobbying disclosure website
section_or_category: 4. How easily can the public access disclosed information? (sub-facet b)
scoring_rule: 0-2 points (sub-component of category 4). 2 = data downloads available. Subjective scoring; partial points implied.
source_quote: Two points each were assigned to user-friendly search and availability of downloadable data. papers/text/OpenSecrets_2022__state_lobbying_disclosure_scorecard.txt:215-216
notes: Sub-facet of public_availability_total. Article rationale: "downloadable data is not just a tool for data geeks, but a valuable asset that allows anyone searching for lobbyist data to capture that information" (lines 127-129). Failure modes include searches without unique URLs, results paginated across many pages (lines 130-134).

indicator_text: Easily accessible lists of registered lobbyists and clients
section_or_category: 4. How easily can the public access disclosed information? (sub-facet c)
scoring_rule: 0-1 points (sub-component of category 4). 1 = list available and easily accessed. Partial points for lists "that are available, but not as easily accessed."
source_quote: Within the five points allocated to this category, one point is dedicated to the availability of lobbyist and client lists. Partial points were assigned based on lists that are available, but not as easily accessed. papers/text/OpenSecrets_2022__state_lobbying_disclosure_scorecard.txt:212-214
notes: Sub-facet of public_availability_total. Rationale: "many of the search systems require users to know the name of a lobbyist or client, easily accessible lists of lobbyists and clients are often a necessity to a functional lobbying transparency site" (lines 120-126). Recommendation: "Easily accessible lists of lobbyists and clients... should be a best practice for all states" (lines 273-276).
